analogy: Think of "Constitution as a Living Document" through the chapter's own opening images: 196 Indian Constitution at Work Chapter Nine CONSTITUTION AS A LIVING DOCUMENT INTRODUCTION In this chapter, you will see how the Constitution has worked in the last 69 years and how India has managed to be governed by the same Constitution. After studying this chapter... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Constitution as a Living Document is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that 196 Indian Constitution at Work Chapter Nine CONSTITUTION AS A LIVING DOCUMENT INTRODUCTION In this chapter, you will see how the Constitution has worked in the last 69 years and how India has managed to be governed by the same Constitution.

The text explains that after studying this chapter you will find out that: ± the Indian Constitution can be amended according to the needs of the time; ± though many such amendments have already taken place, the Constitution has remained intact and its basic premises have not changed; ± the judiciary has played an important role in protecting the Constitution and also in interpreting the Constitution; and ± the Constitution is a document that keeps evolving and responding to changing situations.

The text explains that 197 Chapter 9: Constitution as a Living Document ARE CONSTITUTIONS STATIC?

The text explains that it is not uncommon for nations to rewrite their constitutions in response to changed circumstances or change of ideas within the society or even due to political upheavals.

The text explains that the Soviet Union had four constitutions in its life of 74 years (1918, 1924, 1936 and 1977).

The text explains that in 1991, the rule of the Communist Party of Soviet Union came to an end and soon the Soviet federation disintegrated.

The text explains that after this political upheaval, the newly formed Russian federation adopted a new constitution in 1993.

The text explains that the Constitution of India was adopted on 26 November 1949.

The text explains that its implementation formally started from 26 January 1950.

The text explains that more than 69 years after that, the same constitution continues to function as the framework within which the government of our country operates.

The text explains that is it that our Constitution is so good that it needs no change?

The text explains that was it that our Constitution makers were so farsighted and wise that they had foreseen all the changes that would take place in the future?

The text explains that it is true that we have inherited a very robust Constitution.

The text explains that the basic framework of the Constitution is very much suited to our country.

The text explains that it is also true that the Constitution makers were very farsighted and provided for many solutions for future situations.

The text explains that but no constitution can provide for all eventualities.

The text explains that in the chapter on the Judiciary, you have already studied the issues of difference between the Judiciary and the Parliament: one was the relationship between fundamental rights and directive principles, the other was the scope of right to private property and the third was the scope of Parliament’s power to amend the Constitution.

The text explains that there was strong pressure from the pubic in favour of certain amendments.

The text explains that from the information that many amendments were made during 2000-2003, which of the following conclusions would you draw? a.

The text explains that judiciary did not interfere in the amendments made during this period. b.

The text explains that one political party had a strong majority during this period. c.

The text explains that 219 Chapter 9: Constitution as a Living Document d.

The text explains that there were no real differences among the parties during this time. e.

The text explains that the amendments were of a non-controversial nature and parties had an agreement on the subject of amendments.

The text explains that explain the reason for requiring special majority for amending the Constitution.

The text explains that many amendments to the Constitution of India have been made due to different interpretations upheld by the Judiciary and Parliament.

The text explains that if amending power is with the elected representatives, judiciary should NOT have the power to decide the validity of amendments.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work, Chapter 9: Constitution as a Living Document

prelims_traps: Do not treat CONSTITUTION AS A and LIVING DOCUMENT as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Constitution as a Living Document; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Constitution as a Living Document through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use CONSTITUTION AS A and LIVING DOCUMENT to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
